title: Analysis of the Near Field by the Fast Physical Optics Method with the Adaptive Mesh Technique
authors: Si Jia Guo, Yu Mao Wu, Li-Xin Guo
publish: IEEE Journal on Multiscale and Multiphysics Computational Techniques
abstract: In this work, physical optics (PO) scattered near fields of electrically large target under different excitation sources are calculated by the linear amplitude-based fast physical optics method combined with the adaptive mesh technique (ALFPO). A key innovation is the derivation of phase gradient-based adaptive sampling interval formulas for quadratic patches, applicable to calculation of scattered near and far fields, which addresses the over-sampling issue in PO method. Also, adaptive mesh distribution generated via the phase gradient and local error of scattered field is analyzed in view of high-frequency wave physics. Moreover, the edge grid segmentation (EGS) approach is proposed to smoothen shadowed boundaries, ensuring the more accurate lit region data for calculation. Numerical results demonstrate that both the phase gradient-based ALFPO (PG-ALFPO) and scattered field error-based ALFPO (FE-ALFPO) outperform the linear amplitude-based fast physical optics (LFPO) method and FEKO software. They reduce the number of patches by one or two orders of magnitude while maintaining accuracy. Notably, the PG-ALFPO directly reflects high-frequency physical mechanisms. Both ALFPO methods achieve memory savings at the cost of CPU time, with PG-ALFPO exhibiting nearly twice the efficiency of FE-ALFPO. In short, the ALFPO methods along with EGS approach provide a significant way to solve near-field scattering features of electrically large target.

title: Bioinspired Agile Swimming with a Highly Underactuated Soft Ribbon Fin_supp1-3673909.mp4
authors: Michael Tolley
abstract: Soft autonomous underwater vehicles (AUVs) that employ biological swimming mechanisms have demonstrated immense promise in navigating marine environments with increased efficiency and adaptability and decreased environmental disturbances in comparison to their rigid counterparts. However, most AUVs with soft propulsors are poorly suited for precise swimming at low speeds and station holding tasks—two capabilities critical to underwater monitoring and manipulation. In contrast, gymnotid fish undulate an elongated ribbon fin to aptly swim at low-speeds with high agility and have inspired soft AUVs that mimic their swimming mechanics. However, these AUVs typically use numerous actuators to force undulatory kinematics in their ribbon fin rather than leveraging the fluid-structure interaction between the soft ribbon fin and surrounding water. In this work, we developed a sparsely actuated robot, inspired by the black ghost knifefish (apteronotus albifrons) and capable of highly agile swimming with only two actuated fin rays. Leveraging prior work on the hydrodynamics of ribbon-finned swimming, we constructed a heuristic for expected thrust generation as a function of the ribbon fin's actuation. Using this heuristic, we investigated four parameterized motion primitives for agile in-plane swimming: 1) forward swimming, 2) banked turning maneuvers, 3) pure yaw rotation, and 4) lateral (i.e., sideways) swimming. We developed a single parametric gait to express all of these primitives and experimentally characterized each primitive. Ultimately, we found that by leveraging fluid-structure interaction, our highly underactuated platform could execute the in-plane swimming capabilities necessary for agile locomotion.

title: Three-Dimensional Evaluation of Abnormal Upper Extremity Joint Coupling Post-Stroke
authors: Prabhat Pathak, Sarah K. Cavanagh, James Arnold, Lynn Blaney, Patrick M. Puma, David J. Lin, Conor J. Walsh
publish: IEEE Transactions on Neural Systems and Rehabilitation Engineering
abstract: Stroke-induced abnormal upper extremity (UE) joint coupling limits independent joint control and impairs functional arm use. While previous studies have predominantly evaluated UE joint coupling within the plane of motion (in-plane) and its effect on functional task performance, minimizing unnecessary joint movements outside the plane of motion (out-of-plane) across all UE degrees of freedom (DOFs) is essential for coordinated movements. To address this limitation, we developed an experimental procedure leveraging 3D motion capture to evaluate in- and out-of-plane joint coupling for seven UE DOFs during isolated shoulder, elbow, and wrist joint movements, and functional task performance during an object transfer task. We introduce a method to calculate the in- and out-of-plane joint coupling ratio (JCR) for seven UE DOFs. We investigated the contribution of stroke-induced abnormal in- and out-of-plane joint coupling to explain deficits in functional task performance using hierarchical regression analysis. In 18 individuals post-stroke, joint coupling was abnormal in- and out-of-plane for seven UE DOFs, evident by significantly higher JCR values for the paretic arm compared to the non-paretic arm. The regression model using both in- and out-of-plane joint coupling explained significantly higher variance, up to 33.8%, in stroke-induced deficits in movement duration, hand trajectory smoothness, trunk displacement, hand movement extent, and peak velocity time compared to models only using either in-plane or out-of-plane joint coupling. Our work advances post-stroke abnormal joint coupling evaluation methods across all UE DOFs, required to more comprehensively understand stroke-induced impairments in independent UE joint control and their effect on functional task performance.

title: Integrating Advantage Actor-Critic in Multi-Robot Collaboration_supp1-3674008.mp4
authors: Hao Huang
abstract: Recent advances in large language models (LLMs) have spurred interest in using these models to coordinate multi-agent robot systems. However, existing approaches often fail to handle dynamic and complex environments effectively. We present A2C-Collab, an <underline xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">a</u>dvantage <underline xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">a</u>ctor <underline xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">c</u>ritic framework tailored to multi-robot collaboration. A2C-Collab contains three major components: (1) an actor generates time-critical commands for each robot to execute, and (2) a critic monitors execution and recommends corrections when plans fail. (3) An advantage mechanism verifies these corrections by forecasting their impact on subsequent environmental dynamics. While previous methods primarily relied on the critic to enhance collaboration, they often lacked a verification mechanism, allowing the critic to unintentionally guide agents away from the correct goal. In contrast, our approach introduces an advantage verification stage that anticipates and evaluates the impact of corrective actions before execution, ensuring more reliable and goal-aligned coordination. The framework was first evaluated in RoCoBench, a standard multi-robot simulation, and subsequently deployed to a physical robot cluster. Across both settings, A2C-Collab improved task completion rates compared with the state-of-the-art baselines, demonstrating robust performance and highlighting the promise of LLM-driven reasoning in real-world multi-robot systems.

title: Demand Modeling for Advanced Air Mobility: Challenges, Opportunities, and Future Directions
authors: Kamal Acharya, Waleed Raza, Katherine Vasiloff, Zhenbo Wang, Liang Sun, Houbing Herbert Song
publish: IEEE Transactions on Intelligent Transportation Systems
abstract: Advanced Air Mobility (AAM) has attracted increasing research and industry attention as a potential extension of passenger and cargo transportation systems, supported by developments in electric vertical take-off and landing (eVTOL) aircraft, autonomy, and integrated traffic management. Unlike conventional transportation systems, AAM presents unique challenges to demand modeling due to untested routes, new vehicle classes, and evolving user behavior. This paper conducts a comprehensive survey of demand modeling approaches relevant to AAM, encompassing econometric models, discrete choice frameworks, simulation-based approaches, machine learning techniques, and hybrid methods. Our evaluation focuses on four core modeling stages: trip generation, trip distribution, mode choice, and assignment. We further analyze the key factors influencing demand and assess their implications for model design and forecasting accuracy. Research gaps are identified in areas such as airspace routing, vertiport placement, capacity constraints, weather resilience, and community acceptance. To address these, we highlight the need for infrastructure-aware, behaviorally grounded, and real-time adaptive demand models. We outline future research opportunities including fine-grained temporal and spatial resolution, multimodal integration, induced demand representation, and equity considerations. By categorizing current methods and synthesizing emerging directions, this survey establishes a critical research agenda to guide policymakers, practitioners, and researchers in advancing robust and socially responsible AAM demand forecasting.

title: Robot-Mediated Explainability for CNN-Based Complex Emotion Recognition: Aligning Visual Attributions with Social Robot Behavior for Trust-Aware HRI
authors: Eryka Probierz, Tomasz Grzejszczak
abstract: In recent years, the development of socially interactive robots has heightened the need for interpretable and trustworthy models capable of recognizing complex human emotions in real time. While deep convolutional neural networks achieve strong performance in facial affect recognition, their decision processes remain largely opaque, which limits user trust and safe deployment in human–robot interaction (HRI). This challenge becomes particularly critical when recognizing complex or subtle emotional states under domain shift, where both performance robustness and explanation stability are required. A robot-mediated explainability framework for complex emotion recognition links the visual attribut of a convolutional neural network (CNN) to social-robot behaviors in real time. The perception module augments a CNN with an Attention Map Alignment Layer (AMAL) that regularizes the internal focus toward semantically meaningful facial regions during training. Model-agnostic eXplainable AI (XAI) methods (SHAP and LIME) provide per-instance rationales at inference and are audited for temporal stability and faithfulness under streaming conditions. Explanations are embodied through a behavior-generation layer that converts saliency into deictic head motion and gaze, optionally governed by an adaptive emphasis policy that modulates when and how strongly to highlight evidence as a function of model confidence, explanation stability, and user-state signals. Evaluation spans AffectNet and EMOTIC, plus an out-of-distribution robot-domain set (OhBot) to probe domain shift. A human–robot interaction study uses a 3×2×2 between-subjects design crossing embodiment (no robot, still gaze, head motion), visual XAI (no heatmap vs heatmap), and emphasis policy (fixed vs adaptive). Across in-distribution and robot-domain tests, AMAL improves accuracy and macro-F1 with lower expected calibration error, and yields higher attribution–landmark overlap with greater temporal stability. In interaction, head motion increases acceptance of system recommendations relative to still-gaze and no-robot baselines, while heatmaps improve task performance irrespective of embodiment. Adaptive emphasis delivers additional gains primarily under well-calibrated predictions and attenuates guidance under low confidence, limiting over-reliance. Overall, aligning internal attention, surfacing faithful rationales, and embodying those rationales through socially legible behaviors enhances both objective performance and calibrated trust. Next steps include ondevice deployment on embedded accelerators, longitudinal evaluation of trust dynamics, and integration with language-model-based social-norm explanation modules as a complementary, normative layer.

title: Contour-Based Generation and Composite Attention Diffusion for High-Transmission Metasurface Inverse Design
authors: Zhihai Zheng, Zezhou Zhang, Yanhe Lyu, Zhihao Jiang, Yifeng Qin, Hongbin Li
publish: IEEE Antennas and Wireless Propagation Letters
abstract: Low-emissivity (Low-E) glazing employs conductive coatings that severely attenuate mmWave/Ka signals. This letter presents a compact generative inverse-design framework for high transmission metasurfaces patterned on Low-E glass at 24−30 GHz under dual-angle, dual-polarization constraints while limiting the etched-area ratio. We first build a full-wave validated dataset using a 3D contour-based generator with continuous interpolation and dual-threshold tracing to encode smooth metal air transitions, coupled with NSGA-II multi-objective optimization to efficiently populate sparse high-performance regions. Based on this dataset, we develop MetaDiffusion(Cond Att), a conditional diffusion model with composite attention that tokenizes angular-polarization-resolved S-parameters for accurate spectrum-to-geometry mapping. On the test set, Cond Att reduces the spectrum MAE to 0.036 (surrogate-validated), outperforming a self-attention baseline, and generates diverse manufacturable patterns for wide-angle transmission.

title: Lightweight Remote Sensing Multispectral Image Compression with Decoupled Interactive Knowledge Distillation
authors: Qizhi Fang, Jingang Wang, Jiahui Liu, Lili Zhang
publish: IEEE Journal of Selected Topics in Applied Earth Observations and Remote Sensing
abstract: To address the challenges of spectral and spatial redundancy in multispectral images, as well as the limitations of satellite-to-ground bandwidth and onboard computational resources, a lightweight multispectral image compression with decoupled interactive knowledge distillation framework, MIC-DIKD, is proposed in this paper. Within the encoder-decoder backbone, a Multi-Scale Spatial-Spectral Redundancy reduction block (MSsRr) and a Decoupled Dual-Branch Attention (DDBA) module are introduced. Cross-band and cross-spatial dependencies are jointly modeled. Redundancy is suppressed at the source, and representational capacity is enhanced. Dual constraints at the pixel and feature levels are further applied. The student model is guided toward the teacher. Strong rate-distortion performance and perceptual quality are achieved. Experiments on Landsat-8 and Sentinel-2 show that MIC-DIKD outperforms several baselines in PSNR, MS-SSIM, and MSA. In terms of complexity, the student model distilled from the teacher provides comparable performance. FLOPs and encoding/decoding latency are substantially reduced. The framework is therefore well suited to the engineering requirements of onboard compression and ground-based fast decoding. Overall, a practical solution is provided for multispectral image onboard compression, ground decoding, and large-scale processing. High performance is maintained, and complexity is reduced.

title: A Generalized Algorithm for Retrieving Land Surface Temperature from UAV Thermal Infrared Imagery
authors: Meng Ji, Yongming Xu, Shanyou Zhu, Yan Xin
publish: IEEE Journal of Selected Topics in Applied Earth Observations and Remote Sensing
abstract: Unmanned aerial vehicles (UAVs) provide an effective tool for fine-scale land surface temperature (LST) mapping, but satellite-based algorithms are unsuitable due to their much lower flight altitudes. Currently, few studies have focused on UAV-based LST retrieval methods, and most either neglect atmospheric influences or rely on atmospheric radiative transfer simulations using local atmospheric profiles, limiting general applicability. This study develops a generalized UAV-based LST retrieval (GULR) algorithm for UAV thermal cameras integrating the widely used FLIR Tau 2 thermal core. The relationships between atmospheric transmittance, upwelling and downwelling atmospheric radiance with near-surface meteorological parameters and UAV height were analyzed using simulations based on TIGR profiles. Generalized empirical equations were then developed to estimate these atmospheric parameters from near-surface air temperature, humidity, and UAV height. Correction equations were also developed to minimize uncertainties in the conversion between temperature and radiance induced by the equivalent wavelength of the broad UAV thermal band. Additionally, band-specific land surface emissivity for six major land cover types was provided to facilitate calculation. Subsequently, LST can be retrieved by removing atmosphere and emissivity influences. The algorithm was validated using both simulated datasets and field observations, achieving mean absolute errors (MAEs) of 0.33 K and 1.49 K, respectively. Sensitivity analysis demonstrated the robustness against noise in near-surface meteorological variables and emissivity. This study proposes a generalized, easy-to-use, and accurate UAV-based LST retrieval algorithm, offering valuable technical support for high-resolution thermal environment monitoring.

title: Xiling: Cryo-CMOS Manipulator Using Dual 18-bit R-2R DACs for Single-Electron Transistor at 60 mK
authors: Yingjie Li, Yifei Zhang, Haichuan Lin, Cheng Wang
abstract: Millions of quantum-bits (qubits) are envisioned for a fault-tolerant quantum computer. For scalability, silicon spin qubit stands out due to its compatibility with advanced CMOS processes. Silicon single-electron transistors (SETs) are widely adopted for the quantum state discrimination of spin qubits. For high-fidelity quantum logic gate and readout, the gate electrodes of the above Si quantum devices require a biasing voltage with ~10-<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\mu $</tex-math> </inline-formula>V precision to define the electron occupation, and ultra-low noise to suppress the charge noise induced decoherence. In addition, the co-integration of a large-scale spin qubit array and cryogenic CMOS (Cryo-CMOS) electronics on a <100-mK stage of dilution refrigerator requires ultra-low power (LP). This article presents a 65-nm Cryo-CMOS manipulator integrated with a SET at 60 mK, consuming a DC power of <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$60~\mu $</tex-math> </inline-formula>W only. It adopts a pair of 18-bit R-2R DACs for the gate control. Each DAC has 63 bits of MSB thermal code, 3 bits of MID thermal code, 10 bits of binary code, and 3 bits of fine-tuning code. However, at cryogenic temperature, the P+ polysilicon resistors of the DAC suffer from <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$43\times $</tex-math> </inline-formula> stronger nonlinearity and <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$2\times \sim 3\times $</tex-math> </inline-formula> worse device mismatch of the resistance value, limiting integral nonlinearity (INL) and differential nonlinearity (DNL). Accordingly, resistor nonlinearity calibration (RNC) is proposed to compensate for the voltage dependency of resistance using a pair of tailed pMOS and nMOS transistors. Ordered element matching (OEM) is also adopted to mitigate random mismatch. Measured at 4 K, this chip achieves a state-of-the-art 18-bit resolution with 4.6-<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\mu $</tex-math> </inline-formula>V precision, an INL of ±0.8 LSB, and a DNL of ±0.8 LSB. It also exhibits an integrated noise of <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$3.8~\mu $</tex-math> </inline-formula>V and a noise spectrum density of 4.1 nV/Hz<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">0.5</sup> at 60 mK. Finally, while the two DACs of this chip control the gate and source of the SET, respectively, a clear Coulomb diamond of the SET is measured at 60 mK.

title: Adaptive Step-Size Digital Back-Propagation for High-Capacity Unrepeatered Optical Systems with Raman Amplification
authors: Zhiyuan Yang, Mengfan Fu, Yicheng Xu, Xi Chen, Lilin Yi, Weisheng Hu, Qunbi Zhuge
abstract: In this paper, we propose an adaptive step-size digital back-propagation (AS-DBP) scheme to compensate for fiber nonlinearity in unrepeatered systems with distributed Raman amplifier (DRA). We first design a signal power profile adaptation (SPPA) algorithm to estimate the signal power profile from the DRA gain and link length. Subsequently, a step-size adaptation (SSA) algorithm is employed to optimize the step-sizes and other DBP parameters based on the SPPA results. Furthermore, AS-DBP is extended to subcarrier multiplexing (SCM) systems, considering cross-subcarrier nonlinearity (CSN) compensation. The proposed method is numerically and experimentally evaluated via single-channel dual-polarization 16-ary quadrature amplitude modulation (16-QAM) DRA-amplified unrepeatered transmission, including both single-carrier (SC) and SCM systems. In 240-km first-order and second-order DRA-amplified SC simulation systems with a symbol rate of 200 GBaud, the signal-to-noise ratio (SNR) gains of 12-step AS-DBP are 1.45 dB and 1.50 dB compared with linear compensation (LC), respectively. In 300-GBaud 8-subcarrier SCM systems over a 240-km second-order DRA-amplified unrepeatered simulation link, 12-step AS-DBP achieves 1.99-dB and 0.73-dB SNR gains compared with LC and SCM-DBP, respectively. In 300-GBaud 8-subcarrier SCM systems over a 320-km unrepeatered simulation link employing both remote optically pumped amplifier (ROPA) and second-order DRA, the SNR gains of 16-step AS-DBP are 1.72 dB and 0.80 dB compared with LC and SCM-DBP, respectively. In 185-km 40-GBaud SC and 185-km 40-GBaud 4-subcarrier SCM DRA-amplified unrepeatered experimental systems, AS-DBP obtains SNR gains of 0.38 dB and 0.21 dB compared with conventional DBP, respectively.

title: A Novel Source Separation Chaotic Optical Communication Architecture Based on Deep Cascade Time-delay Reservoir Computing
authors: Pengjin Zhu, Hongxiang Wang, Yuefeng Ji
abstract: In this paper, we propose and experimentally investigate a novel source separation chaotic optical communication architecture based on optical Reservoir Computing (RC). Benefiting from the RC's powerful high-dimensional mapping capabilities, the well-trained RC can replace the traditional chaotic receiver to achieve high-quality separation decryption without chaos synchronization, which significantly reduces costs and challenges. Specifically, the RC adopted in the proposed scheme has a deep cascade electro-optical feedback (DCEO) structure, which effectively enriches the reservoir state response. Numerical results show that the NMSE of the separated chaotic carrier is 0.0078 and the Q-factor of the separated OOK signal is 9.239, which achieves satisfactory decryption results. Simultaneously, the experimental and numerical conclusions are well verified with each other, which reflects the desirable reproducibility and reliability of the proposed scheme. Furthermore, the DCEO-RC system was compared with the other 3 RCs, and it had the lowest NMSE and the highest Q-factor in both experiments and numerical simulations, which represents the best separation performance. Subsequently, the impact of mask coefficient, SNR, chaotic system configurations, and RC parameters on separation performance was analyzed in detail. Finally, we analyze communication transmission quality. The results show that 20GBaud OOK/QPSK signals can achieve normal communication over 200 km. In summary, the proposed scheme is expected to provide strong support for high-speed chaotic secure communication.

title: Adaptive Orchestration of Semantic-Computation Resources in Network-Edge-Robot IIoT
authors: Bingxin Tian, Han Meng, Ruoguang Li, Lei Wang, Zijun Huang, Zhenning Huang, Bin Wei, Hao Zhang, Zhu Han
abstract: The advancement of the embodied AI-enhanced Industrial Internet of Things (IIoT) increasingly relies on high-efficiency communication under the limited bandwidth of private factory networks. Semantic communication has shown potential in enhancing transmission efficiency by extracting task-relevant information, yet effectively coordinating semantic compression and computational resources in such resource-constrained environments remains a major challenge. This paper formulates the integrated optimization of semantic compression levels and computing resource allocation as a dynamic multi-agent decision problem. We propose an attention-based multi-agent deep reinforcement learning (MADRL) framework, which enables distributed robots to adaptively adjust semantic encoding strategies and resource usage in a coordinated manner. Simulation results demonstrate that the proposed approach significantly outperforms benchmark methods, achieving a 20% reduction in communication latency and an 13% improvement in task success rate under typical industrial network conditions.

title: Disentangling Global Orientation with Test-Time Rectification for Unsupervised Non-rigid Point Cloud Correspondence_supp1-3668605.pdf
authors: Jiaqi Yang
abstract: Unsupervised non-rigid point cloud correspondence aims to identify dense matches between deformable point cloud pairs without ground truth annotations. While existing methods effectively handle shape deformations, they often struggle under complex global orientation changes involving large rotation angles and arbitrary rotation axes. In this paper, we propose a robust test-time point cloud correspondence method that disentangles complex orientation changes from non-rigid shape deformation. We achieve this by modeling global orientation changes as rigid transformations via deformation-invariant priors from pre-trained models to disentangle complex orientation changes while remaining insensitive to shape deformation. Moreover, to resolve symmetry ambiguities induced by complex orientation changes, we augment point clouds with rotational symmetry transformations along learned axes to capture subtle disambiguating cues. To evaluate the impact of arbitrary and complex orientation changes, we construct corrupted versions of existing datasets encompassing varying rotation scales and axes. Extensive experiments reveal the effectiveness of our proposed method.

title: Mapless Underwater Navigation using Convex Quadratic Programming based Geomagnetic-Inertial Fusion
authors: Qianyun Zhang, Youmin Zhang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Instrumentation and Measurement
abstract: This paper presents a geomagnetic-inertial fusion navigation method that addresses heading ambiguity caused by gradient vanishing in mapless navigation scenarios. The key innovation lies in reformulating the fusion navigation problem in underwater environment without prior geomagnetic maps as a convex quadratic programming (CQP) optimization approach, fundamentally transforming the traditional Kalman filter-based sensor fusion into a constrained model predictive control (MPC) scheme. This CQP formulation enables the direct integration of target geomagnetic information while ensuring computational efficiency and global optimality. Crucially, we introduce a dynamic weighting mechanism, driven by an anomaly detector, which allows the system to intelligently select reliable magnetic data and revert to inertial navigation in distorted fields. This ensures high robustness, computational efficiency, and real-time performance. The proposed method facilitates robust multi-destination navigation without requiring precise initial parameter calibration and effectively mitigates cumulative drift during long-range navigation. Comprehensive validations through Monte Carlo simulations and BlueROV2-based experiments demonstrates substantial improvements: 81.9% and 84.8% improvements in circular error probable 50% (CEP<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">50</sub>) and CEP90 accuracy compared to traditional geomagnetic navigation, and 56.1% and 57.4% improvements compared to standalone inertial navigation, respectively.

title: Hybrid C-LSTM Neural Architecture for Real-time Adaptive Control of Fixed-Wing UAVs in Variable Turbulence Conditions
authors: Liguo Tan, Yongcheng Xiong, Guanghui Wen, Kangkang Sun, Keyou You
publish: IEEE Transactions on Automation Science and Engineering
abstract: Fixed-wing unmanned aerial vehicles (UAVs) performing turn maneuvers in turbulent environments face significant control challenges due to stochastic aerodynamic disturbances. This paper proposes a hybrid turbulence-aware control framework that integrates mathematical modeling, global optimization, and machine learning for adaptive autopilot gain tuning. A semi-invariant-based deterministic reduction of the stochastic lateral-directional model is developed, reducing the dimensionality from 44 to 7 equations while preserving control-relevant statistical characteristics. To determine optimal feedback gains, a Modified Survival of the Fittest Algorithm (MSoFA) is introduced, demonstrating improved global convergence reliability and achieving 5–15% better solution quality compared to conventional evolutionary methods. An offline-generated dataset of optimal gains across varying turbulence intensities and flight altitudes is then used to construct a clustered Long Short-Term Memory (C-LSTM) architecture that combines DBSCAN-based regime identification with recurrent neural approximation. The proposed neural controller achieves 98.3% gain prediction accuracy and reduces computation time by more than three orders of magnitude relative to direct optimization. Extensive numerical simulations confirm stable turn performance over altitudes ranging from 800 to 1500 m and turbulence scales from 150 to 470 m, with terminal yaw-rate deviations below 0.01%. The novelty of the proposed framework lies in the structured integration of (i) semi-invariant-based stochastic model reduction, (ii) globally reliable evolutionary optimization for constrained gain tuning, and (iii) regime-dependent recurrent neural approximation for real-time implementation. To the best of the authors’ knowledge, such an integrated turbulence-aware tuning framework for fixed-wing UAV autopilots has not been previously reported.

title: Integrating Behavioral Data and Explainable AI for Predicting Young Adult Depression
authors: Mumtahina Islam Khan, Sayed Munim Shahriar, Khaled Ahmed, Arif Hasan, Md Jalal Uddin Chowdhury, Md Masuduzzaman
publish: 2026 5th International Conference on Electrical, Computer &amp; Telecommunication Engineering (ICECTE)
abstract: The primary goal of this research is to estimate the likelihood of depression in young adults with the help of machine learning (ML) methods and discuss the chance of early diagnosis. The dataset used in the study was on Kaggle and consisted of about 27,901 records that had different social and lifestyle-related factors as academic pressure, sleep duration, and financial stress. Having finished data preprocessing, feature selection (LASSO and Random Forest) and class balancing (SMOTE and Random Undersampling) twelve machine learning models were trained and assessed. The results showed that the CatBoost model was the most accurate ( Accuracy = 0.845 ) and F1-score (0.869) model, as it performs better than other models. There was also the development of an ensemble model which gave more stable predictions. In order to make the decision-making process of the model transparent, the techniques of Explainable Artificial Intelligence (Explainable AI) were accepted, and SHAP analysis was performed to identify the impact of the most significant features. The results of the current study can be used to build a data-driven solution to mental health monitoring, early risk detection, and future in the clinical or social support system.

title: Ergodicity and Perturbation Bounds for a Finite Source Computer Queueing Network
authors: Svetlana Vostokova, Yana Sergienko, Alexander Zeifman
publish: 2025 9th International Conference on Information, Control, and Communication Technologies (ICCT)
abstract: In this article, an approach to studying non-stationary finite-source queueing systems is presented. An estimate of the rate of convergence and stability of the original process was obtained, which enables the analysis of system behavior under time-dependent conditions. The method for obtaining the convergence rate estimate is based on the concept of the logarithmic norm of an operator function and Cauchy operator estimates. The study of stability is based on the concept of exponential ergodicity. A numerical example illustrates the behavior of the continuous-time system; graphs are constructed showing the probability of an empty queue and the average number of customers in the system for specific intensity functions $\lambda(t), \mu(t)$. Keyword-Finite-source queueing systems, bounds on the rate of convergence, Markov chains, logarithmic norm method, exponential ergodicity, stability

title: Design and Development of Optical Sensor Emulator for Verification of on-Board HDA Algorithm of Chandrayaan-3
authors: Vikas Singh, Shivani Bhargav, Umang Bhatia, Shitisha Bajpai, Pramod Mishra, Tanna Jaimin Hasmukhray, Abhishek Kunal, Ritesh Kumar Sharma, B Saravana Kumar
publish: 2025 IEEE Microwaves, Antennas, and Propagation Conference (MAPCON)
abstract: This paper presents the design and development of an Optical Sensor Emulator (OSE) intended for the verification of the Hazard Detection and Avoidance Processor used in the Chandrayaan-2 and Chandrayaan-3 missions. The OSE is designed to transmit simulated lunar image data to the Data Processing Unit (DPU) via a designated interface, facilitating the testing and validation of the Hazard Detection and Avoidance (HDA)'s functionality within the DPU. The development of the OSE involved the use of VHDL based FPGA design, MATLAB for image pre-processing, LabVIEW for graphical user interface, and <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{C}$</tex> based software module for data transfer over USB interface. The emulator was realized on a custom Kintex-7 FPGA-based mother board with LVDS, USB daughter cards and serves as part of a ground checkout unit. It interfaces with a host PC via USB and connects to the DPU through a dedicated LVDS interface.

title: Data-Driven Model Updating and Load Identification for Rotor Blades Using Neural Networks
authors: Ye Wei, Mengwei Lu, Zhenguo Zhang, Zhongliang Xie, Shiming Zhang
publish: 2025 Global Reliability and Prognostics and Health Management Conference (PHM-Xian)
abstract: Accurate identification of distributed loads on helicopter blades based on feasible sensor measurements plays a crucial role in enhancing overall flight performance and ensuring operational safety. In this study, a novel approach based on Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) networks is proposed to construct a surrogate model for distributed force reconstruction. Unlike conventional regularization methods, the proposed framework enables reliable recovery of time-varying spatial load distributions without excessive dependence on parameter tuning or steady-state assumptions. Moreover, as a prerequisite for load identification, the rotor blade model is accurately updated using a Deep Neural Network (DNN). The methodology is validated on the well-established XH-59A rotor blade model, demonstrating the effectiveness of neural networks in both model correction and distributed load identification.

title: Deep Learning and Probabilistic Clustering for Anomaly Detection in Nuclear Power Plants
authors: Jinxiao Yuan, Shuang Lin, Lin Guo, Sui Fan
publish: 2025 Global Reliability and Prognostics and Health Management Conference (PHM-Xian)
abstract: Anomaly detection in nuclear power plants (NPPs) is critical for ensuring operational safety and preventing potential failures. Traditional monitoring methods usually involve waiting for anomalies to reach a threshold limit before triggering corresponding alarms, by which time an accident has typically already occurred. With artificial intelligence, however, we can handle high-dimensional data and capture complex nonlinear relationships, enabling us to detect anomalies much faster. This paper proposes a hybrid approach combining Stacked Autoencoders (SAEs) with Gaussian Mixture Models (GMM) to improve anomaly detection performance. The SAEs is employed to extract hierarchical features, followed by a Gaussian Mixture Model (GMM) optimized through a combined scoring metric integrating Bayesian Information Criterion (BIC), Akaike Information Criterion (AIC), and silhouette coefficient for probabilistic clustering of latent features. A dual-threshold strategy based on reconstruction error and cluster distance is then applied to identify anomalies. Experimental results demonstrate that the proposed method effectively detects anomalies in NPP, exhibiting higher robustness and accuracy compared to single-method approaches. The framework not only enhances early fault diagnosis but also provides interpretable insights into anomaly sources, contributing to safer and more efficient nuclear power plant management.

title: Enhanced Localization of Driverless Trains using Multi-Scale Grid LiDAR Fiducial Marker Features*
authors: Pandey Jyotsna, Kenji Imamoto, Taku Shimizu
publish: 2025 IEEE 28th International Conference on Intelligent Transportation Systems (ITSC)
abstract: This paper presents a comprehensive investigation into the driverless train localization technique employing fiducial markers, enhanced by LiDAR sensor technology. The primary contribution lies in the development of a novel multi-scale grid fiducial marker LiDAR template encoding methodology, aimed at improving detection robustness across diverse environmental conditions across multiple ranges. By encoding templates at multiple scales, the proposed approach accommodates variations in marker appearance, sparseness, and orientation, thereby enhancing detection accuracy and reliability by LiDAR. Furthermore, the study delves into the impact of fiducial marker LiDAR template selection based on the distance from the LiDAR sensor, elucidating its influence on detection performance. In this study, a novel approach for train localization using LiDAR technology and ArUco fiducial markers is proposed. The method addresses the challenges inherent in comparing structured image-based fiducial markers with unstructured LiDAR data. By generating ArUco templates from LiDAR data and employing multi-scale grid score decoding, successful marker identification and localization up to 50 meters are achieved. The findings demonstrate the feasibility and effectiveness of utilizing LiDAR for fiducial marker-based train localization, paving the way for enhanced railway navigation systems.

title: Hybrid Deep Learning-based Solar Irradiance Forecasting for Matarbari Coastal Region—The Next Generation Power Hub
authors: Shekh Mohammed Noumanul Islam, Nur Mohammad
publish: 2026 5th International Conference on Electrical, Computer &amp; Telecommunication Engineering (ICECTE)
abstract: Accurate prediction of solar irradiance is crucial for integrating solar power into power grids to enhance photovoltaic (PV) performance, particularly in regions with fluctuating and non-stationary weather conditions. This paper presents a hybrid deep learning model designed for Matarbari, Cox’s Bazar, Bangladesh, a coastal area characterized by highly variable and unpredictable climatic behavior. The proposed framework integrates an Artificial Neural Network (ANN) with a Bidirectional Long Short-Term Memory (BiLSTM) network to capture complex nonlinear relationships and temporal dependencies from high-resolution Solcast irradiance and meteorological data. By combining the feature-extraction capability of ANN with the sequence-learning strengths of BiLSTM, the hybrid model achieves improved forecasting accuracy and reliability. The model was trained and evaluated on multivariate coastal datasets and demonstrated strong generalization across a wide range of climate conditions. The proposed ANN-BILSTM hybrid model achieved exceptional performance on the test set, yielding a Coefficient of determination (R<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup>) of 0.9768, a Root Mean Square Error of 0.0304, and a Mean Absolute Error of 0.0240 on scaled data. Overall, the proposed approach provides a robust data-driven forecasting solution that supports reliable solarpower integration, strengthens grid operational planning, and contributes to long-term energy management for Matarbari’s future power infrastructure.

title: The Emotional Signature: Detecting AI-Generated Short Stories through Narrative and Emotion Analysis
authors: Shahriar Arefin Zummon, Atqiya Bushra Khan, Tasnim Ahmed Mim, Samiya Taher Sumiya
publish: 2026 5th International Conference on Electrical, Computer &amp; Telecommunication Engineering (ICECTE)
abstract: The proliferation of AI-generated content poses serious problems for copyright protection and content authentication, especially for narrative-length documents. Character development, plot coherence, thematic consistency, and emotional authenticity are just a few of the intricate narrative nuances that must be captured for story-level detection, which is far more difficult to model than short-text classification tasks. These finer distinctions cut across dozens of tokens. In this paper, we conduct a systematic in-depth study to detect AI-generated stories by exploiting multi-task learning framework with emotion classification. We experiment with several machine learning and deep learning models on a dataset of short-length Bangla stories, including the traditional classifiers (SVM, Random Forest, Decision Tree and Naïve Bayes) as well as state-of-the-art transformer-based models (BERT-multilingual, XLM-RoBERTa, DistilBERT and BanglaBERT). Our multi-task framework simultaneously classifies source (AI vs. Human) and emotion (6 categories), achieving 100% accuracy on source classification using BanglaBERT and 88.14% on emotion detection. The experimental results demonstrate that transformer-based models significantly outperform traditional machine learning approaches, with BanglaBERT achieving the best combined performance (F1-score: 0.9403). This work contributes to the emerging field of AI-generated content detection at the narrative level and provides insights into the characteristics that distinguish AI-written from human-written stories, with particular attention to how narrative-level linguistic patterns differ from surface-level text features.

title: A Novel Framework for Fall of Ground Risk Estimation Using Contactless GPR Imagery
authors: Amit Swain, Anwesha Khasnobish, Chirabrata Bhaumik, Tapas Chakravarty, Aishik Pal, M Jaleel Akhtar
publish: 2025 IEEE Microwaves, Antennas, and Propagation Conference (MAPCON)
abstract: The structural integrity of any underground mine during the operation relies heavily upon the stability of its surrounding rockmass. This is often compromised by the presence of subsurface discontinuities that remain concealed within the rock, leading to fall of ground (FoG) accidents and fatalities. Conventional FoG risk assessments rely heavily on invasive core drilling methods and laboratory-based rockmass quality determination. This work explores a contactless GPR based approach to image thin geological features of the rockmass by accurately modeling the environment, and proposes a framework to estimate zone-wise FoG risk index directly from the resulting image reconstruction. An extended phase shift migration algorithm is also presented to enhance the downrange resolution using a sparse deconvolution technique. Lastly, a zone-wise FoG risk index is estimated by segmenting the reconstructed image, yielding the least RMSE of 2.76 with respect to ground-truth.

title: Multi-Agent Phishing Detection And Deletion via Small VLM and LLM Reasoning
authors: Fateha Jannat Ayrin, Mizanur Rahman, Ashifur Rahman, Vishwanath Akuthota, Abhijit Kumar Ghosh, Md Maruf Hasan Khondaker, Muntasim Ul Haque, Montaser Abdul Quader, Md Tanzim Reza
publish: 2026 5th International Conference on Electrical, Computer &amp; Telecommunication Engineering (ICECTE)
abstract: Phishing attacks remain one of the most pervasive cybersecurity threats, with email-based phishing responsible for over 90% of global data breaches. Traditional text-based detection systems achieve moderate success in identifying linguistic cues but fail to capture visual deception strategies such as brand impersonation, domain typosquatting, and counterfeit login layouts. This paper introduces an autonomous multi-agent framework that leverages reasoning capable Vision-Language Models (VLMs) for end-to-end phishing detection and remediation within Gmail environments. The proposed agent continuously monitors spam folders, analyzes rendered email screenshots, and autonomously executes remediation actions permanently deleting confirmed phishing messages (confidence ≥ 0.80) or restoring legitimate ones to the inbox. Experiments evaluate reasoning VLMs (Qwen2-VL-2B, Qwen2.5-VL-7B) against standard architectures (LLaVA-1.6, Moondream2, Phi-3.5-Vision) across 150 real-world phishing samples. Results show that reasoning-enhanced VLMs achieve 82–85% detection accuracy, reducing false positives by approximately 15% compared to standard VLMs (58–63% accuracy). Furthermore, integrating a lightweight reasoning LLM (Qwen-3-4B-Thinking) improves multimodal consistency, yielding an additional 3% accuracy gain and greater semantic alignment between visual and textual cues. This study advances the field of agentic AI for cybersecurity by demonstrating the practicality of fully autonomous, multimodal phishing email management on commodity hardware. The complete implementation publicly available at: https://github.com/rafi79/AI-Phishing-Detector.

title: Broadband Emissivity Analysis of Black Body Target Materials for Calibration of Michelson Interferometer Diagnostic
authors: Abhishek Sinha, Abdul Shabir, Surya Kumar Pathak, Kumar Ajay
publish: 2025 IEEE Microwaves, Antennas, and Propagation Conference (MAPCON)
abstract: The Michelson interferometer diagnostic at the SST-1 tokamak (IPR) measures the electron cyclotron emission (ECE) spectrum to reconstruct the plasma electron temperature profile. Accurate calibration of this system requires black body sources at different temperatures. To select these, four commercial radar absorbing materials (RAM), namely — TK THz RAM Large and Small Tiles, Eccosorb AN-72, and AN-73, were characterized over 75–220 GHz using a Vector Network Analyzer. Monostatic transmission and reflection measurements were used to compute emissivity based on energy conservation. The TK THz RAM Large Tile demonstrated superior performance, with emissivity exceeding 0.99 across most frequencies. This analysis identifies suitable materials for selecting reliable black body sources for ECE calibration.

title: Transforming Closed-Set to Open-Set Diagnosis: Placeholder Learning for Bearing Faults
authors: Chuanmeng Wang, Tongxiao Yang, Zhirui Kai, Xinpeng Wang, Rui Yang, Zenghui An
publish: 2025 Global Reliability and Prognostics and Health Management Conference (PHM-Xian)
abstract: In industrial applications, bearing fault detection is essential for ensuring machinery reliability and preventing costly, catastrophic failures. Traditional fault diagnosis models rely on a closed-set assumption, meaning all fault types encountered during testing are predefined. However, real-world operations often present novel fault types, which can be misclassified and consequently diminish system reliability. To address this challenge, we propose a novel open-set bearing fault detection framework, drawing inspiration from the concept of learning placeholders. Our approach leverages data placeholders, created via manifold mixup techniques, to simulate potential unknown fault patterns. This effectively transforms a closed-set training process into an open-set one. Additionally, classifier placeholders act as adaptive thresholds to dynamically discriminate between known and unknown faults. Experiments on benchmark bearing fault datasets demonstrate the superior performance of our method, which significantly improves the detection of unknown faults while maintaining high accuracy for known ones. The proposed framework provides a practical and robust solution for bearing fault diagnosis in dynamic industrial environments.

title: Factors Influencing the usage of Cloud Storage Services: A Case Study in an Indonesian Private University
authors: Justin Joe Adwidjaja, Steven Ong, Wahyu, Yulius Lie
publish: 2025 5th International Conference on Information Communication and Software Engineering (ICICSE)
abstract: The growth of cloud storage and its usage has increased over the years and was further increased by the COVID-19 pandemic. Cloud storage is widely used to support daily activities, including those in higher education in Indonesia. This study explores the factors of cloud storage usage, specifically Microsoft OneDrive, in higher education in Indonesia. Using the Technology Acceptance Model (TAM) with modifications and a quantitative research approach, the following six key factors were analyzed: Knowledge Sharing (KS), Social Influence (SI), Perceived Usefulness (PU), Perceived Ease of Use (PEOU), Attitude Towards (AT), and Intention to Use (ITU). Data was collected from 418 respondents who actively use Microsoft OneDrive through an online survey and analyzed using SmartPLS. The results show that students are more likely to actively use Microsoft OneDrive if they find it beneficial, particularly for academic activities.

title: GPU-Accelerated Barrier-Rate Guided MPPI Control for Tractor-Trailer Systems
authors: Keyvan Majd, Hardik Parwana, Bardh Hoxha, Steven Hong, Hideki Okamoto, Georgios Fainekos
publish: 2025 IEEE 28th International Conference on Intelligent Transportation Systems (ITSC)
abstract: Articulated vehicles such as tractor-trailers, yard trucks, and similar platforms must often reverse and maneuver in cluttered spaces where pedestrians are present. We present how Barrier-Rate guided Model Predictive Path Integral (BR-MPPI) control can solve navigation in such challenging environments. BR-MPPI embeds Control Barrier Function (CBF) constraints directly into the path-integral update. By steering the importance-sampling distribution toward collisionfree, dynamically feasible trajectories, BR-MPPI enhances the exploration strength of MPPI and improves robustness of resulting trajectories. The method is evaluated in the highfidelity CarMaker simulator on a 12 [m] tractor-trailer tasked with reverse and forward parking in a parking lot. BR-MPPI computes control inputs in above <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$100[~\text{Hz}]$</tex> on a single GPU (for scenarios with eight obstacles) and maintains better parking clearance than a standard MPPI baseline and an MPPI with collision cost baseline.

title: A Highly Selective High Gain Vivaldi Filtenna with Multiple Radiation Nulls for UWB, Satellite Communication and Short Range Radar Applications
authors: Abhishek Patel, Trivesh Kumar, Manoj Singh Parihar
publish: 2025 IEEE Microwaves, Antennas, and Propagation Conference (MAPCON)
abstract: In this paper, an Ultrawideband Vivaldi Filtenna (UVF) has been realized by integration of multi-mode resonator (MMR) and a highly selective low pass filter (LPF). The MMR helps to create two radiation null (RN), one at lower cut-off while another at upper cut-off of the operating band. The measured results show 10 dB impedance bandwidth (IBW) of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$106 \%(3.5 \text{GHz}-11.5 \text{GHz})$</tex> and almost a flat pass band gain with maximum value of 5 dBi. Three radiation nulls with suppression level more than 20 dB below the maximum gain are obtained with lower and upper edge selectivity of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$0.03 / 0.02$</tex> respectively. All the parameters are in good reconciliation with simulated results that pronounce the proposed UVF as a potential candidate for the modern RF front end systems, ultrawideband (UWB) and Radar applications.

title: Boosting Credit Card Fraud Detection Through XGBoost and Balanced Data
authors: Md. Muktar Hossain, Mobassir Ahmmed, Sirazum Munira Mim, Md. Munem Shahriar, Tanver Ahmed
publish: 2026 5th International Conference on Electrical, Computer &amp; Telecommunication Engineering (ICECTE)
abstract: Fraudulent use of payment cards poses a serious legal violation, severely damaging personal credit profiles and banking entities. Detecting fraudulent credit card activity remains a persistent issue for financial systems due to the low occurrence rate of fraud and the necessity for swift, reliable identification. Conventional detection techniques tend to be inefficient, demanding significant manual effort and time. This study presents a novel framework that integrates an advanced boosting algorithm with data balancing strategies to enhance classification effectiveness on a skewed dataset. An extensive evaluation was conducted using labeled transaction records to assess the efficacy of various classification models, including Support Vector Machine (SVM), Gaussian Naïve Bayes (GNB), Decision Trees (DT), Adaptive Boosting Regression (ABR), Logistic Regression (LR), and XGBoost. The research also explores how performance metrics are influenced by oversampling, feature refinement, and hyperparameter adjustments. The results show that the SVM, ABR, LR, GNB, DT, and XGBoost classifiers have an overall accuracy (OA) of 99.95%, 99.92%, 99.95%, 97.89%, 99.93%, and 99.96%, respectively. Findings indicate that the proposed hybrid model, particularly when employing the XGBoost algorithm with synthetic data expansion, delivers superior results compared to standard classifiers. Metrics such as precision, recall, and F1-score consistently favor this method, highlighting its advantage in identifying rare fraudulent cases with remarkable accuracy.

title: Impact of Missing Inter-Beat Intervals on Heart Rate Variability Features and Driver Drowsiness Detection
authors: Aristotelis Styanidis, André Lourenço, Carlos Carreiras, Christer Ahlström, Apostolos Ziakopoulos, George Yannis
publish: 2025 IEEE 28th International Conference on Intelligent Transportation Systems (ITSC)
abstract: Drowsiness detection using physiological data, particularly heart rate variability (HRV), has emerged as a promising approach for assessing driver fatigue. Inter-beat intervals (IBIs) represent the time between consecutive heartbeats and are used to derive HRV, a measure of autonomic nervous system activity. HRV can be extracted from electrocardiogram (ECG), photoplethysmography (PPG) or other physiological signals, making it valuable for applications in health monitoring and driver state assessment. However, real-world conditions often lead to missing or corrupted data due to inconsistent sensor contact, motion artifacts, or signal interruptions. Such data loss can impact HRV feature extraction and affect downstream machine learning (ML) models. This study investigates the effects of missing data by systematically removing <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$15-30 \%$</tex> of IBIs, either randomly or in sequential blocks. The distribution analysis indicated that IBI distributions largely retain their overall structure, with only minor deviations. Classification performance was robust to the investigated data losses, but when comparing 10-fold cross-validation to leave-one-participant-out validation, mean accuracy dropped by approximately 15%, and variability in accuracy across folds increased by around 10%. tSNE feature space visualization further revealed that class separability was much clearer at the participant level than at the group level. The findings underscore the need for personalized models tailored to each driver's physiological patterns, which has implications for drowsiness detection systems.

title: Health Condition Prediction of Power Diodes Using Particle Filter
authors: Chao Guo, Zhonghai Lu
publish: 2025 Global Reliability and Prognostics and Health Management Conference (PHM-Xian)
abstract: This paper proposes the application of Particle Filter (PF) for predicting the health condition of power diodes. The dynamic adjustability of PF based on measurements enables consistent performance on devices with significant variations in lifetimes. Evaluated on the aging dataset of power diodes where forward voltage data serves as the precursor signal, PF achieves consistent superior performance on all 6 devices compared to the traditional Kalman Filter, with an average Mean Squared Error (MSE) improvement of 68.29% and an average Mean Absolute Error (MAE) improvement of 46.73%. Additionally, we explore performance across a broad design space to investigate trade-offs between performance, robustness, and computational cost. A high process and measurement noise ratio (Q/R), i.e., low measurement noise relative to process noise, improves accuracy but reduces robustness, while increasing the number of particles enhances both accuracy and robustness at the expense of higher computational cost. By selecting appropriate Q/R ratios and particle numbers, the robustness is ensured while effectively balancing performance with computational cost.

title: Development of High Voltage Test Bench for High Power mm Wave Gyrotron Testing
authors: Awadhesh Kumar Baranwal, Naveen Kumar Sharma, Tapas Ranjan Swain, Nani Medicherla, Ranjan Kumar Parida, Ramray Hansdah, P Sidharthan, Santanu Karmakar, S K Datta
publish: 2025 IEEE Microwaves, Antennas, and Propagation Conference (MAPCON)
abstract: This paper describes development of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\sim 300 ~\text{kW}$</tex> compact Test bench for testing of high power mm wave Gyrotron at MTRDC, DRDO. A configurable high voltage test bench has been designed and developed using a capacitive energy storage mechanism to meet the long pulse width required for Gyrotron up to <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{1 0 ~ m s}$</tex>. This test bench consists of the high energy storage capacitor, a modular solid state semiconductor IGBT based high voltage switch, necessary protection and monitoring circuitary along with Communication and control Circuitary for enabling the remote operation. An indigenous high power mm wave Gyrotron has been designed and developed in house at MTRDC catering the 100 kW RF output power. The high voltage test bench has been tested with dummy load and Gyrotron tube enabling the RF power to prove its functionality. The experimental setup along-with results for <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$50 \text{kV}, 6 ~\mathrm{A}$</tex>, upto 5 ms pulse width has been shown in subsequent section of this paper.

title: Deep-Koopman-Based Model Predictive Control for Trajectory Tracking of Automated Vehicle
authors: Lei Li, Liwei Xu, Zilong Liu, Tingyuan Wang, Ang Li, Guodong Yin
publish: 2025 IEEE 28th International Conference on Intelligent Transportation Systems (ITSC)
abstract: This paper proposes a novel trajectory tracking model predictive control (MPC) method leveraging deep neural network-based Koopman operator theory. Specifically, the approach employs a deep-learning-enhanced Extended Dynamic Mode Decomposition (Deep EDMD) framework, integrating encoder-decoder architectures to automatically learn nonlinear lifting and projection functions for vehicle dynamics. The resulting linear representation significantly simplifies control computations. Extensive simulations in a high-fidelity CarSim/Matlab environment demonstrate robust tracking performance under dynamic curvature scenarios, with maximum lateral displacement errors remaining within ISO 3888-2 standards. Comparative analyses reveal that the proposed method substantially outperforms traditional MPC, exhibiting improved stability, tracking accuracy, and adaptability under varying adhesion coefficients and vehicle speeds.

title: Large Language Models for Autonomous Driving: A Review of Technical Challenges, Datasets, and Simulation Environments
authors: Shokouh S. Ahmadi, Lingxi Li
publish: 2025 IEEE 28th International Conference on Intelligent Transportation Systems (ITSC)
abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs) have emerged as transformative tools in artificial intelligence, primarily known for their capabilities in natural language understanding and generation. Recently, their potential to enhance autonomous driving (AD) systems has garnered growing interest. By introducing interpretability, human-like reasoning, and flexible interaction mechanisms, LLMs offer promising solutions to long-standing challenges in the AD domain, including the lack of transparency, common-sense decision-making, and intuitive human-machine interaction. However, their integration into AD systems presents new concerns related to data quality, prompt engineering, safety, and reliability in real-time environments. This paper provides a comprehensive overview of the current landscape of LLMs in autonomous driving, outlining the main contributions, existing challenges, benchmark datasets, and simulation tools. We also highlight future research directions to bridge the gap between natural language intelligence and autonomous vehicle safety and performance.

title: Contrast & Compress: Learning Lightweight Embeddings for Short Trajectories
authors: Abhishek Vivekanandan, Christian Hubschneider, J. Marius Zöllner
publish: 2025 IEEE 28th International Conference on Intelligent Transportation Systems (ITSC)
abstract: The ability to retrieve semantically and directionally similar short-range trajectories with both accuracy and efficiency is foundational for downstream applications such as motion forecasting and autonomous navigation. However, prevailing approaches often depend on computationally intensive heuristics or latent anchor representations that lack interpretability and controllability. In this work, we propose a novel framework for learning fixed-dimensional embeddings for short trajectories by leveraging a Transformer encoder trained with a contrastive triplet loss that emphasize the importance of discriminative feature spaces for trajectory data. We analyze the influence of Cosine and FFT-based similarity metrics within the contrastive learning paradigm, with a focus on capturing the nuanced directional intent that characterizes short-term maneuvers. Our empirical evaluation on the Argoverse 2 dataset demonstrates that embeddings shaped by Cosine similarity objectives yield superior clustering of trajectories by both semantic and directional attributes, outperforming FFT-based baselines in retrieval tasks. Notably, we show that compact Transformer architectures, even with low-dimensional embeddings achieve a compelling balance between retrieval performance (minADE, minFDE) and computational overhead, aligning with the growing demand for scalable and interpretable motion priors in real-time systems. The resulting embeddings provide a compact, semantically meaningful, and efficient representation of trajectory data, offering a robust alternative to heuristic similarity measures and paving the way for more transparent and controllable motion forecasting pipelines.

title: CNC Machining and Characterization of W-Band Folded Waveguide Structures
authors: Santosh Kumar, Anurag Srivastava, Sreekuttan R, N Venkaiah, S K Datta, M V Kartikeyan
publish: 2025 IEEE Microwaves, Antennas, and Propagation Conference (MAPCON)
abstract: This paper presents CNC milling fabrication process for W-band folded waveguide (FWG) interaction structures at a center operating frequency in W-band. Interaction structures or slow-wave structures (SWS) are the most complex mechanical assemblies of a Travelling Wave Tube (TWT) in the sub-THz regime. FWG interaction structures made of two different materials were investigated for their dimensional and RF characteristics. This study primarily focuses on investigating the possibility of using conventional machining technology for the development of FWG interaction structures in the lower end of the sub-THz frequency regime. A high-precision CNC milling machine was used to fabricate the FWG structures. The machining parameters for the selected cutting tool and workpiece materials were optimized for the optimal surface roughness (Ra) required of the machined FWG. The experimental cold test <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{R F}$</tex> measurement responses were found to be close to the simulated results.

title: A Highly Sensitive and Optimized SPR Biosensor for Monitoring Polluted Water Samples
authors: Kazi Taskir Tanzid, Mohammed Ashfaq Alavee, Ashfaque Ahmed, Md. Asibur Rahman Bappy, Riyaj Ahmed Siyam, Khandakar Mohammad Ishtiak
publish: 2026 5th International Conference on Electrical, Computer &amp; Telecommunication Engineering (ICECTE)
abstract: In this work, a highly sensitive and enhanced multi-layered SPR biosensor structure has been developed, which is capable of detecting polluted water samples effectively. The designed sensor operates according to the principle of Kretschmann configuration, and the structure contains five layers, including the prism. The materials and their respective optimized thickness selected for the sensor layers are BK7 (prism)/TiO<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</inf> (23 nm)/Cu (42 nm)/Si3N<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">4</inf> (18 nm)/BaTiO<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">3</inf> (3 nm)/Sensing Medium. The sensor demonstrates high performance by delivering a sensitivity of 474.60 (deg/RIU). At the same time, it also offers a high QF of 113.26 (1/RIU), FOM of 110.29, DA of 0.305 (1/deg), and a reduced minimum reflectance (Rmin). The parameters obtained give an indication that the sensor is able to determine the water pollutants as well as the variation in salinity in a complex aquatic environment efficiently.

title: Smart Marketing Chatbot of Amazon Office Products Using Generative Based Sequence to Sequence Model
authors: Alaa T. Al-Tuama, Dhamyaa A. Nasrawi
publish: 2026 5th International Conference on Electrical, Computer &amp; Telecommunication Engineering (ICECTE)
abstract: More businesses are switching to automated procedures from traditional customer service as a result of the accelerated advancement of technology. Additionally, The need for the use of conversational agents has skyrocketed. Bots that can carry on natural conversations that use natural language to mimic human dialogue. Additionally, consumers can pose inquiries or issue commands to obtain the information or service they require in a conversational and natural manner. Moreover, consumers require assistance when purchasing products, and the provision of this service requires a substantial amount of work and energy. This research aims to improve a smart marketing chatbot by employing a generated-based strategy to mitigate the likelihood of answer defects, as real-time responses are provided. A sequence-to-sequence LSTM model was employed to construct a smart marketing chatbot that is both effective and well-suited for generative chatbot tasks. Question-and-answer Amazon office products comprise the dataset employed in this investigation. Three experiments are implemented in this investigation to optimize chatbot functionality. The BLEU score and cross entropy loss are employed to assess the models. The results indicate that the chatbot appropriately addressed user inquiries, resulting in a training loss of 0.004 and a validation loss of 3.01. The optimal model achieved a BLEU score of 0.57. The proposed chatbot model is compared to other related chatbots in the English dataset; our model outperformed the others when queries were posed with natural variations.

title: Advancing Simulation-Based Optimization with LLMs for Traffic Problems
authors: Xin Liu, Jinbiao Huo, Zhen Zhou, Zhitao Hu, Ziyuan Gu, Zhiyuan Liu
publish: 2025 IEEE 28th International Conference on Intelligent Transportation Systems (ITSC)
abstract: This paper introduces a novel framework for addressing expensive-to-evaluate simulation-based optimization (SBO) problems in traffic systems by incorporating the global reasoning capabilities of large language models (LLMs). Existing SBO methods commonly rely on random, data-driven search strategies that lack physical interpretability and are often in-efficient—particularly during the early stages of optimization. To address this limitation, we design an LLM-assisted Bayesian Optimization (BO) framework in which the LLM acts as a domain expert, providing global search suggestions informed by simulation outputs and problem-specific context. Specifically, the proposed framework incorporates LLM-inferred knowledge to dynamically define a high-quality sampling region. This region is then explored using conventional BO guided by acquisition functions. We demonstrate the effectiveness of this approach using a traffic signal control problem. Experimental results highlight two key benefits: (1) the LLM-enhanced BO identifies high-quality solutions earlier in the optimization process, and (2) it improves the stability of the optimization by mitigating premature convergence. Overall, this work introduces a generalizable and extensible methodology for embedding domain knowledge into simulation-based optimization through LLMs, offering a new paradigm for traffic system decision-making.

title: Introducing OASISS: ODD-Based AI Safety in AutonomouS Systems
authors: Jerein Jeyachandran, Siddartha Khastgir, Xingyu Zhao, Eric Barbier, Paul Jennings
publish: 2025 IEEE 28th International Conference on Intelligent Transportation Systems (ITSC)
abstract: The rise in automation across industries is leading to machines increasingly performing functions that render human intervention obsolete. Artificial Intelligence (AI) unlocks the machine's ability to reach higher levels of autonomy by carrying out the tasks of reasoning and planning based on its perceived surroundings. With AI becoming increasingly responsible for safety-critical functions, it is imperative for manufacturers, regulators and certification authorities to ensure these functions are performed safely. Scenario-based methods have emerged as an effective solution to train and test the AI model of autonomous systems. It allows the system to be exposed to the various operational conditions it may encounter once deployed into the real world. However, this raises the question of whether the operational conditions considered for the training and testing of the AI model are truly sufficient. OASISS (ODD-based AI Safety In autonomouS Systems) aims to quantitatively assess the adequacy of the training and testing data of an AI model in relation to its targeted area of operation. The OASISS framework uncovers the gaps that are prevalent in the current scenario-based training and testing landscape and provides an evaluation mechanism guided by the dataset-related safety properties outlined in ISO PAS 8800.

title: C-Band 6-Bit Digital Phase Shifter MMIC Design for Space-Borne Synthetic Aperture Radar
authors: Mounika Kare, Ravi Khatri, Piyush Sinha, MukeshKumar K Patel, Ch V Narasimha Rao
publish: 2025 IEEE Microwaves, Antennas, and Propagation Conference (MAPCON)
abstract: This paper presents the design of a 6-bit digital phase shifter (DPS) MMIC for C-Band synthetic aperture radar application. The phase shifter is fabricated using a 0.25 -um pseudomorphic-High Electron Mobility Transistor (p-HEMT) GaAs process from the UMS foundry, France. The phase-shifting circuits are designed using delay line and switched filter topologies. Measured results show an RMS phase error less than 5 deg, insertion loss better than 5.2 dB, and return losses better than 10 dB across all 64 states in the frequency range of 5.2-6 GHz. The chip size is <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$4 \text{mm} \times 2 \text{mm}$</tex>, including driver circuits for all the bits.

title: Conditioned Trajectory Generation for Realistic Driving Scenarios via a Hybrid Machine Learning Architecture
authors: Robin Egolf, Alexander Fertig, Michael Botsch
publish: 2025 IEEE 28th International Conference on Intelligent Transportation Systems (ITSC)
abstract: Simulation is considered a critical component in the validation of autonomous driving systems, particularly for evaluating rare and safety-critical scenarios. While recent trajectory prediction methods have shown strong capabilities in generating realistic future trajectories, these models are typically designed to estimate the most likely outcome and do not support explicit conditioning on scenario parameters. As a result, their use in targeted simulation or scenario design remains limited. To address this, a framework for conditioned trajectory generation is presented, enabling the generation of a set of diverse and physically plausible trajectories from a single initial scenario state, conditioned on interpretable highlevel variables such as desired endpoints or average velocity. A hybrid machine learning framework is employed to generate control actions, which are subsequently processed by a kinematic motion model. Expert knowledge is incorporated through soft physical constraints and a smoothness loss, ensuring physical realism and signal continuity. Through experiments, it is demonstrated that realistic and diverse trajectories aligned with scenario context can be produced. The proposed framework can be integrated into simulation environments for validating autonomous vehicles.

title: Concurrent Multiple Entanglement Routing Model with Function-Configurable Nodes in Quantum Networks
authors: Ryuta Shiraki, Bijoy Chand Chatterjee, Eiji Oki
publish: 2025 IEEE International Conference on Advanced Networks and Telecommunications Systems (ANTS)
abstract: In traditional quantum networks, transmitter/receiver (TR) and repeater (REP) roles in quantum networks are assigned to separate physical nodes due to distinct hardware requirements However, advances in integrated quantum processors now enable nodes to switch between these roles. This paper proposes a quantum network model for concurrent entanglement routing that exploits this capability, allowing each node to function flexibly as both a TR and a REP within a multilayered graph framework. The model is formulated as an optimization problem aimed at maximizing the number of successful entanglement connections for a given set of requests. Logical TR and REP roles within the same physical node share quantum memory and fiber resources, leading to more efficient resource utilization. Numerical experiments show that the proposed model offers improved routing flexibility and better resource efficiency compared to existing benchmark models.

title: A Unified MATLAB/Simulink Framework for Comparative Control of Grid-Connected Electric Vehicles
authors: Samiul Al Zami Alif, Md. Nazmul Howlader, Bishowjit Paul, Khadiza Akter, Asfaqul Islam, Asaduzzaman Rhine
publish: 2026 5th International Conference on Electrical, Computer &amp; Telecommunication Engineering (ICECTE)
abstract: The swift deployment of electric vehicles (EVs) into the contemporary power systems requires complex control techniques that can facilitate the provision of two-way power flow, grid stability, and consistent charging processes. Available literature tends to test controllers in isolated and non-uniform simulation, which does not allow a fair comparison. This paper introduces a single, combined, MATLAB /Simulink-based EV-grid framework of five uniform control strategies, Proportional-Integral (PI), Proportional-Derivative (PD), Proportional-Integral-Derivative (PID), Sliding Mode Control (SMC) and Model Predictive Control (MPC), via a single fixed EV-grid plant framework. A standardized converter constraint and performance metric multi-controller switching architecture with plug-and-play interface allows reproducible benchmarking of performance at the same operating conditions. The simulation findings show that MPC is superior in dynamic response, steady-state regulation as well as low grid current harmonic distortion whereas SMC is robust at the cost of higher oscillatory behavior. PID has a balanced performance at nominal conditions. The suggested system is expandable and can be used in future controller development, hardware-in-the-loop testing, and EV-grid interoperability studies.Keywords—Grid-connected electric vehicles, vehicle-to-grid (V2G), controller benchmarking, predictive control, robust control, MATLAB/Simulink.

title: Consensus-Aware AV Behavior: Trade-Offs Between Safety, Interaction, and Performance in Mixed Urban Traffic
authors: Mohammad Elayan, Wissam Kontar
publish: 2025 IEEE 28th International Conference on Intelligent Transportation Systems (ITSC)
abstract: Transportation systems have long been shaped by complexity and heterogeneity, driven by the interdependency of agent actions and traffic outcomes. The deployment of automated vehicles (AVs) in such systems introduces a new challenge: achieving consensus across safety, interaction quality, and traffic performance. In this work, we position consensus as a fundamental property of the traffic system and aim to quantify it. We use high-resolution trajectory data from the Third Generation Simulation (TGSIM) dataset to empirically analyze AV and human-driven vehicle (HDV) behavior at a signalized urban intersection and around vulnerable road users (VRUs). Key metrics, including Time-to-Collision (TTC), Post-Encroachment Time (PET), deceleration patterns, headways, and string stability, are evaluated across the three performance dimensions. Results show that full consensus across safety, interaction, and performance is rare, with only <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{1. 6 6 \%}$</tex> of AV-VRU interaction frames meeting all three conditions. These findings highlight the need for AV models that explicitly balance multi-dimensional performance in mixed-traffic environments.
